coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of graphene quantum dots and silver nanoparticles compounds with excellent surface enhanced Raman scattering performance
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Juan
Li, Yan
Wang, Jun
Pu, Yang
Xue, Wendong
Liu, Xiaoguang
coredata.dc:description: Here, silica (SiO2) supported silver nano-particles (Ag NPs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) compounds were designed and synthesized via a “green” photochemical approach and an electrophoresis deposition technique in order to provide a highly active surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate. In this approach, the electrochemically prepared aqueous solution of GQDs was used as a solvent and a reducing agent to synthesize in-situ Ag-GQDs compounds under Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. These compounds were collected on a SiO2 supported Si substrate through the electrophoresis deposition technique. Benefiting from their proper size (1–4 nm) and distribution in the spatial gaps between adjacent Ag NPs, GQDs could act as “hot spot” sites for lighting up the Raman scattering signals. Together with the enhanced adsorption of Rhodamine 6G (R6G) molecules through π-π stacking, the electrostatic interactions from GQDs, and the enlarged specific surface area provided by the SiO2 template, the as-prepared substrate exhibited a strong SERS signal with excellent reproducibility. The detection limit of R6G was pushed to 8.0 × 10−14 M. We hope our work provides a time-saving and facile approach for the design and creation of ultrasensitive SERS substrate for trace species detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photochemical Approach
Ag-GQDs Compounds
Electrophoresis Deposition
Surface Enhanced Raman Scattering

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorbance spectra of (a) GQDs and (b–i) Ag-GQDs colloidal solutions synthesized with different volume ratios of AgNO3:GQD solution (b) 5 μl:2 ml, (c) 10 μl:2 ml, (d) 15 μl:2 ml, (e) 20 μl:2 ml, (f) 30 μl:2 ml, (g) 40 μl:2 ml (h) 50 μl:2 ml, and (i) 60 μl:2 ml; The inset shows the GQDs and Ag-GQDs compounds solutions corresponding to curves a and h, respectively.
(a) TEM image of Ag-GQDs compounds, (b) a high magnification selected from the sample, (c) HRTEM image of Ag-GQDs compounds.
SEM images of (a) SiO2 and (b) Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si composite microspheres, (c) EDS spectrum of Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si composite microspheres.
Raman spectra of 8.0 × 10−6 M R6G adsorbed on (a) Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si, (b) Ag/SiO2/Si, (c) Ag-GQDs/Si, (d) Ag/Si and (e) Si substrates excited by 514.5 nm.

Raman spectra of R6G with different concentrations adsorbed on the Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si substrate excited by 514.5 nm. The concentrations of R6G from bottom to up are 8.0 × 10−14 M, 8.0 × 10−12 M, 8.0 × 10−10 M, 8.0 × 10−8 M, and 8.0 × 10−6 M, respectively.
SERS spectra of R6G solution (8 × 10−8 M) from 12 points randomly selected within a whole substrate area.
A scheme of the fabrication process for the formation of Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si substrate.
The calculated SERS EFs for the different R6G bands based on the Ag/Si substrate.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of graphene quantum dots and silver nanoparticles compounds with excellent surface enhanced Raman scattering performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, silica (SiO2) supported silver nano-particles (Ag NPs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) compounds were designed and synthesized via a “green” photochemical approach and an electrophoresis deposition technique in order to provide a highly active surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate. In this approach, the electrochemically prepared aqueous solution of GQDs was used as a solvent and a reducing agent to synthesize in-situ Ag-GQDs compounds under Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. These compounds were collected on a SiO2 supported Si substrate through the electrophoresis deposition technique. Benefiting from their proper size (1–4 nm) and distribution in the spatial gaps between adjacent Ag NPs, GQDs could act as “hot spot” sites for lighting up the Raman scattering signals. Together with the enhanced adsorption of Rhodamine 6G (R6G) molecules through π-π stacking, the electrostatic interactions from GQDs, and the enlarged specific surface area provided by the SiO2 template, the as-prepared substrate exhibited a strong SERS signal with excellent reproducibility. The detection limit of R6G was pushed to 8.0 × 10−14 M. We hope our work provides a time-saving and facile approach for the design and creation of ultrasensitive SERS substrate for trace species detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) technique, as an ultra-sensitive, nondestructive, and trace species detection technique, has gained significant attention in recent years. This technique could not only provide the intrinsic structural information of probe molecules, but also detect low-concentration adsorbed molecules even single layer molecule [1–3], which makes it possible for SERS to be used in biological, chemical agents detection, monitoring environmental contaminants, and many other areas [4]. SERS substrate as an important prerequisite for this technique has been continuously designed and optimized. In order to fabricate a SERS substrate with high-activity, various common noble metal (eg. Ag, Au, Cu) nanostructures with an electromagnetic enhancement mechanism (EEM) [5–7] have been explored because their localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect, especially the embedded nanogaps (within sub-10 nm), namely “hot spots”, can dramatically amplify the Raman signal of probe molecules if they can be placed in a close distance [8]. Among the investigated noble metals, Ag nanomaterials are one of the most outstanding star materials due to their broad plasmon resonance in the visible region, high signal enhancement and stability, as well as easy preparation [9]. Therefore, many nanostructured Ag substrates were designed and investigated, such as Ag nanodendrites [10], Ag nanorods with ordered arrays [11–13], Ag nanoplates [14]. Recently, Hung-Ying Chen et al. proved that ligand-regulated AgNP superlattices can be applied to achieve highly enhanced (>1.2 × 107) and uniform Raman measurements (<5% variation) over a large area (>1 cm2) [15]. Besides the high enhancement factor (106–108 [16]), it is important for a high-activity SERS substrate to ensure effective and homogeneous capture of target molecules by its surface [12,17]. However, it is usually a challenge for nanostructured Ag substrates to achieve such effective capture because of the weak affinity of some target molecules toward the metal surface [18].

Fortunately, graphene and their derivates [19] have also promoted the development of SERS technique via a chemical enhancement mechanism (CEM), originating from the charge transfer between the graphene and the target molecules [20]. Especially the graphene derivates seem to be excellent SERS supporting materials because their large surface area and oxygen-containing functional groups are favorable for efficient adsorption of target molecules through π–π stacking and electrostatic interactions [21]. To further improve SERS-activity, a series of composite SERS-substrates with Ag nanostructures decorated on graphene derivates frameworks have been prepared, including RGO-AgNP hybrid film [9], Ag NP self-assembled Ag NP-graphene oxide (GO) nanocomposite [22], controlled deposition of Ag nanoplates on GO nanosheets [23], Ag dendrites/reduced graphene oxide (AgD/RGO) composites [24]. However, only part of the probe molecules can really reach to the “hot spots” sites due to the location uncertainty of Ag NPs on GO/RGO and the random distribution of “hot spots” on the composite SERS substrate. Therefore, it is highly desirable to further optimize these composite substrates.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new member of zero-dimensional single or few-layer graphene derivates, have a diameter of only several nanometers. Our previous work showed that the electrochemically prepared GQDs consist of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups on their surfaces, and thus have the ability to reduce Ag cations under Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation [25]. The resultant Ag NPs are formed near the surface of GQDs, so it is easy to assemble Ag-GQDs compounds in solution because of the so-close distance and electrostatic interactions between them. If we can collect these compounds in a properly prepared roughened surface, GQDs with a proper size of 1–4 nm will distribute in the spatial gaps between Ag NPs where abundant “hot spots” will be consequently created. Moreover, the surface oxygen functional groups and the corresponding electronegativity of GQDs, as well as the intrinsic π conjugated structure, will guide the probe molecules precisely to localize and capture in the “hot spot” sites. Both efficient charge transfer between GQDs and probe molecules [26–28] and the creation of abundant “hot spots” will result in a higher SERS signal of probe molecules. Therefore, in the present work, the above promising substrates were fabricated via a photochemical reaction and an electrophoresis deposition process. As expected, they exhibit high sensitivity and reproducibility of SERS signal and push the detect limitation of R6G down to 8.0 × 10−14 M.

An aqueous GQDs (0.1 mg/ml) was prepared via an electrochemical cyclic voltammetry technique as our previous report [29]. Next, a series of AgNO3 fresh aqueous solutions (0.1 mol/L) with measured volumes (5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 μl) were added to GQD solutions (2 ml), respectively, accompanied with stirring for 1 h to form a homogeneous solution of Ag+-GQDs. Subsequently, these resulting mixtures were subjected to UV irradiation at a wavelength of 365 nm (18 W) with a distance of 20 mm for 3 h. After the completion of the photochemical reaction, the color of the solutions became light-yellowish to brownish-red (depending on the volume ratio of GQDs with Ag+ ions), which indicated the formation of Ag NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the samples for SERS study
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic illustration of the preparation procedure is shown in Scheme 1 . According to it, a Si wafer with a size of 20 mm × 5 mm was sequentially cleaned in the mixture solution of H2SO4 (mass fraction is 98%) and H2O2 with a volume ratio of 2:1 and distilled water. Then several layers of uniformly close-packed SiO2 colloidal microspheres (with a size of 200 nm) were assembled on the above cleaned Si substrate via a self-assembly technique [30]. Subsequently, the electrophoresis deposition was carried out under the constant potential mode with an applied potential of 3 V, for about 10 h at room temperature. In this process, the self-assembled SiO2/Si wafer was immersed into the as-prepared Ag-GQDs colloidal solution (about 10 ml) to serve as a working electrode with a working area of 5 mm × 5 mm. Pt and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. After that, the deposited sample was taken out from the electrolyte solution and rinsed softly by demonized water several times and then dried with nitrogen flow for characterization and Raman spectral measurements. A wide range of R6G solutions with different concentrations (8.0 × 10−6 to 8.0 × 10−14 M) were prepared for the SERS measurements.

UV–vis spectroscopy was carried out using a UNIC UV-2800 spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and high resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of Ag-GQDs were obtained on H-7650B electron microscope and JEM-100CXⅡelectron microscope, respectively. The SEM images were achieved on a 7401F scanning electron microscopy. Raman spectra were measured under ambient condition using a LabRAM HR Evolution with a 514.5 nm laser. The laser beam was focused by a 50× (NA = 0.75) objective lens resulting in a spot size of around 1.25 μm in diameter. The acquisition time was 10 s for collecting each spectrum. R6G molecules were adsorbed on the Ag-GQDs and Ag deposited SiO2 surfaces or Ag-GQDs NPs and Ag dendrites films by simply soaking them in the R6G aqueous solution for 1 h and the free molecules were washed with the corresponding solvent and then dried under flowing N2 gas. A reference sample of Si substrate was also prepared for the Raman characterizations after drying.

The UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy first demonstrates the formation of Ag NPs in the aqueous solution of GQDs. In Fig. 1 (a), a sharp characteristic peak at ca. 228 nm and several other broad absorption bands at ca. 270 nm, 305 nm and 370 nm can be observed for GQDs. The sharp one is corresponded to the π–π* transition of carbon–carbon bonds, whereas the rest ones arise from the n–π* transition of carbon-oxygen bonds. The appearance of those broad absorption bands well documents that the electrochemically prepared GQDs contain oxygen functional groups on either side of its basal plane and at the sheet edges [31,32]. After adding AgNO3 aqueous solutions with different volume ratios into the GQDs aqueous solution and subsequent continuous UV exposure of the mixture solutions, a strong absorption band centered at ca. 430 nm can be observed (Fig. 1(b–i)), originating from the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Ag NPs with the size of 30 nm [33]. This indicates that the Ag cations have been reduced to Ag NPs after the UV exposure process. Further observation shows that the intensity of the SPR band of Ag NPs monotonically enhances with the increase of the volume of Ag cations in the mixed solutions from 5 μl to 50 μl, but the position of the SPR band remains almost the same. Because the SPR position is strongly sensitive to the size of noble metal NPs [11,34], we speculate that the more of Ag cations provide, the more of Ag NPs form rather than the larger size of them after the photochemical reaction. The reason can be ascribed to the distribution and inhibition effects of GQDs. Specifically countless GQDs with uniform monodispersity provide the anchor sites for Ag cations in the mixed solution, so more Ag cores with uniform distribution will be formed under UV irradiation when increasing the amount of AgNO3. On the other hand, considering the Ag cores formed near the surface of GQDs, the presence of GQDs also inhibits the growth of Ag cores, thus Ag NPs with similar size are formed and surrounded by GQDs as shown in the TEM images (Fig. 2 a). When the volume ratio between AgNO3 and GQDs solutions reaches 60 μl:2 ml, the position of SPR is the same as before but the intensity decreases. This implies that GQDs have the ability to reduce AgNO3 into Ag under certain molar ratio of GQDs to AgNO3, but part of AgNO3 will remain in their cations state when excessive AgNO3 is added. Based on the above results, we choose the volume ratio of 50 μl:2 ml for producing Ag-GQDs compounds for further characterizations and experiments. The inset of Fig. 1 shows the color change from colorless GQD solution to brownish-red Ag-GQDs colloidal solution after UV irradiation at this volume ratio.

The TEM images further confirm the formation of Ag-GQDs compounds. Fig. 2(a and b) shows an amount of isolated spherical NPs with the size of 10–30 nm surrounded by many smaller NPs (1–4 nm). We speculate that the larger NPs are Ag NPs because their sizes agreed well with the size of Ag NPs reflected from the SPR band in the UV–vis spectra, whereas the smaller ones are GQDs considering the similar size shown in their TEM images (Fig. S1). The HRTEM image (Fig. 2(c)) further verifies this hypothesis and demonstrates good crystalline of Ag NPs and GQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm corresponding to the lattice fringe of the (111) plane of Ag crystallite, while 0.33 nm corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite. In addition, the HRTEM image also reveals clearly that the GQDs and Ag NPs remain close to each other, promising that the GQDs (as small as 1–4 nm) distributed between Ag NPs can play the role of “hot spot” for SERS enhancement.

The self-assembled SiO2 colloidal microspheres on a cleaned Si substrate are shown in Fig. 3 (a), demonstrating a layer structure with unified configuration. The close-packed SiO2 microspheres substrate is then immersed into the Ag-GQDs colloidal solution for electrodepositing Ag-GQDs colloidal NPs under an applied voltage of 3 V. The relatively high electric field provides the driving force for the Ag-GQDs' motion and deposition despite a concomitant electrostatic repulsion being present. In order to reflect more details for the surface morphology, the sample is not carbon or gold spraying treated prior to observation. After electrodepositing, the original smooth surface of the SiO2 microspheres become rough because many nanoscaled particles with the size varying from 10 to 30 nm (Fig. 3(b)) obviously cover their surface, along with aggregations occasionally seen. The EDS analysis (Fig. 3(c)) corresponded to this region shows Ag, C, O and Si emission peaks, confirming the accumulated nanoscaled particles are Ag-GQDs compounds.

To determine the detect limitation of Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si substrate, a concentration-dependent SERS measurement of R6G from 8.0 × 10−6 to 8.0 × 10−14 M with a concentration interval of 10−2 had been performed, the result is shown in Fig. 5 . Similar to an earlier report [39], the Raman signals of R6G have high intensity if the solution concentration is 8.0 × 10−6 M, but they decrease when decreasing the concentration of R6G. Although the intensity of Raman signals is very weak, the characteristic bands of R6G are still clearly visible when the solution is diluted to 8.0 × 10−14 M. This result indicates that the as-prepared Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si substrate is an effective and excellent substrate option for SERS.

Additionally, reproducibility is an important parameter for an applicable SERS substrate. Therefore, we randomly choose 12 separated points on the whole substrate area of 0.25 cm2 to investigate their reproducibility under the same experimental condition. As shown in Fig. 6 , there are no obvious changes in the characteristic peak position except for very minimal variations in the intensity at various locations for the 12 random points. In order to further examine the reproducibility of the substrate, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) in SERS intensity at three prominent peaks at 1358, 1507, and 1647 cm−1 for the R6G concentration of 8.0 × 10−8 M are calculated. The values are 10.12%, 9.78%, 4.27%, respectively, which demonstrate excellent reproducibility of the prepared Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si substrate.

In summary, an Ag-GQDs/SiO2/Si substrate with excellent SERS performance has been fabricated via a photochemical approach and electrophoresis deposition technology. This substrate pushes the detect limitation to 8.0 × 10−14 M of R6G molecules and exhibits high reproducibility. The improved SERS performance of this substrate can be attributed to the synergistic effect of GQDs and SiO2 template. The introduction of GQDs in the substrate plays multiple roles: (i) working as a solvent and a reducing agent; (ii) creating more “hot spot” sites in Ag-GQDs compounds benefiting from their proper size ranged from 1 to 4 nm; (iii) favoring the adsorption of R6G molecules because of their π-π layer structure in nature and electronegativity. In addition, the subtle rough surface provided by SiO2 template has effectively increased the surface area of Ag-GQDs substrate, and thus further guarantee the adsorption of R6G molecules. We hope our work can afford a novel direction to build a new type SERS substrate to trace determination.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51202011), and Beijing Organization department outstanding talented person project (2013D009006000001) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRF-TP-15-005A3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2015.12.055.
